speaker: interviewer
text: No illusion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything filter through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look. You have to understand. And it's very difficult to understand. For a normal mind, living under normal human circumstances, one is richer, one is poorer, one is fatter, one is thinner, but they're all normal circumstances. To understand the mentality of a group that was told they are doomed to die, and couldn't believe that we have to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you'd done--

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd done nothing wrong, socially. We are not criminals rejected by the society. There was no distinction because-- between poor and rich, orthodox and secular, educated and ignorant. All Jews have to die because Jew is not allowed to live. And you just can't see yourself in the group of people who are going to die. So you hope. For what you hope, that the war will finish before they will have time to implement it. We knew that the ultimate is total destruction. At that time, we were not sure what form the total destruction will take, or is taking. There were rumors we were-- which were unacceptable to our human mind. We didn't have a point of reference. Our grandparents, and our parents remember German, remember that in certain areas, they were acting violently, brutally, but they were civilized people. I mean, the concept that they can murder people was impossible. We believed that we might be starved to a point that we will die from starvation, that the starvation can be such that it can incubate a disease, an epidemic, from which you can die. That we will be deprived of medication, of saving ourselves. But to take people, to murder them, was inconceivable. Inconceivable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, your father, with his trips to Germany, saw a change. Your parents were in a very comfortable position.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very comfortable position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, at any time, it enter your head to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. In 1938, he suggested to my mother that we move to Israel, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to them, you can go. I finish my school here in Poland. I was a big fish in a small pond. Why should I put myself at a disadvantage? Go to a country, I don't know the language. English. Or Hebrew was not adequate for my position. You know, that there were others who spoke better. And my mother had a sister there. And, for my mother, there were argument that there are Arabs, and Arab pogroms. That my sis-- my auntie's doing her washing by herself, that the climate is hot, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and you both rejected the thought of going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but my father wasn't very insistent, either, apparently. Because knowing my father, if he wanted something to pull through, he did. But in this case, he-- They discussed it at the dinner table one evening, and they could have gone. I could have been left with one of my many aunts, for a year, or not quite a year. After all, I finished matric in June, 1939. The war broke out in August. But that's beside the point. The point is that what happened to the German Jews, it happened because they were of Polish origin, but we are Polish Jews, and nobody's going to chase us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me just ask you. You were talking about Alfred Rossner. Did you also work for him at any stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, that's why I initiated the proceedings to give him the status of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Righteous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of righteous. I knew him very well. I worked very closely with him. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do, and for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a cutting room. I think I started work in 1941, in a cutting room. And I was in the section that was compiling work for tailors. They were getting bundles with pre-cut work with every part of the uniform included. I made millions of mistake, because counting was never my strong card. And I was not particularly good at these things. I spoke very good German. We all spoke German, because we also studied German at school. And working in his shop put me in touch with a, a class of Jewish people that I never knew before. And if I knew them, I always knew them from the position of being the benefactor. And I found myself in a very disadvantaged position. I didn't speak Yiddish, although my parents, among themselves, spoke Yiddish. They spoke Polish, too. To us, they spoke only Polish. But I, I was surrounded by Yiddish. One of my grandmothers never learned Polish, and never bothered to learn Polish. She spoke Yiddish. Maybe she thought it's Polish. I don't know. But she was very comfortable. She didn't need Polish. The shops in our house were owned by Jews. The newspaper that she read was Jewish. Her housekeeper was Jewish. Her children were Jewish. So she lived in a-- in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: In an environment that she needed no other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Absolutely. A whole environ-- she probably never realize that the Russians left Poland. But-- and that's it. So, so I heard Yiddish. And I can't say that Yiddish was an alien language. But I couldn't speak it. I knew certain expressions. I couldn't conduct a conversation in Yiddish. And these people with whom I work, the real tailors, the real shoemaker, the real Jewish proletariat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spoke--

speaker: interviewee
text: The core of Jewish workforce--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: --spoke Yiddish. And they called me the intelligentsia, and by word, they didn't mean it as a compliment. Far from it. They made me suffer. They-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I might be better educated than them, but I can't sew a button. I might come from a better home than them, but I can't pedal on the machine. And these were the vital things that were needed for survival. Work. And I was in a very disadvantaged position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either they called me the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means the one who finished school, who matriculated, or the intelligentsia. Never by my name. And everything above my head, you know? So I, I was very hurt. I was-- the roles have changed from a position of advantage, in my childhood, in my early youth. I suddenly found myself in a position of disadvantage-- of disadvantage when it came to skills of survival, to skills of real life. But they, the people you were working with there, the Jews you were-- the Jews you were working with there, they weren't cruel to you. They--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They teased you like mad.

speaker: interviewee
text: They teased me. They were not cruel. They came to me with jokes that had a double-meaning that I wasn't supposed-- they-- and they-- but they did not accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always on the periphery of them. And I was hurt. And I came one day home, and I said to my father, I want to learn Yiddish. And as I knew Hebrew, I knew the alphabet. And my father found, somewhere, somebody who was a Bundist and had children's books. And he brought me home children's books. So when I saw a dog, and I saw letters, and I knew the sound of it, so I knew a hunt, even if I-- before I read the word. And that's how I learned Yiddish. Up till today, I could not speak Yiddish fluently, as I speak any other language. I could not continue, and sustain, in conversation in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could know-- you, you understood everything in the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I understood everything in there. And that-- they were terrible times for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work in Alfred Rossner's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, I'll say on the safe side, it would be 1941, at the beginning, till the liquidation of the factory, which was in June, '44. And by that time, Alfred Rossner was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was arrested. Why did they arrest a, a German in that manner?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they arrested him because-- you see, the culture of those times, being inside or outside of ghetto, had one principle, bribe. German were the-- Germans were the most bribable people. You could bribe them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The- the upper echelon, or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Any echelon. There's no-

speaker: interviewer
text: --or the German in uniform? Or you had to go for the officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, the German who were accessible, if you didn't have directly an access to the upper echelon, you started to bribe on the lower level till your bribe came up to the upper echelon. A mere mortal couldn't go up to the chief of Gestapo and say, I give you a bottle of whiskey, and don't put my name on the next transport. You couldn't do that. So, on our behalf, so to say, Rossner was doing the bribing. His factory depended on government orders. And government was SS. And SS actually employed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he so favorable to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because he showed them a true, human, and humane interest. He spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why? It was so rare, and yet, as you said, he employed 6,000--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know much about his background, but I think that he came from a town, and he worked for Jewish families who had factories there, and they actually brought him to Bedzin. He also, politically, was on the left. And, with all things combined, and there could have been, even, a love story in his life with a Jewish girl. I am not quite familiar about it. The Jews from Bedzin who brought him were of German-- were one of the German refugees. And when they brought him here, they knew his abilities. A, that he's dedicated friend of Jews. Definitely not a Nazi. And then, that he has got the skills of organizing. And he's got the know-how how to get the orders. And because he was disabled, and he was totally outside the fighting force of Germany, outside wearing a uniform, the Germans wouldn't want a disabled person to wear a SS uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: It went against their principle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So keep--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he set himself up, in his own word, to serve his beloved country instead with a gun in his hand, to serve his beloved country on the field of war economy. And here he's surrounded by Jews who have got all the skills, and all the willingness, and there are our boys on the Russian front, freezing, and you can have them, and you can do with them what you want, but let us first clad our boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why, in '44, was he arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liquidation of ghettos, all over Europe, started in '43, because that was the time where the Final Solution was implemented. Just shows you their priorities. Never mind the Germans who froze to death on the Russian front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why would they arrest one of their own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, why? Because he was the one who lavishly entertained the SS men. Who offered their wives best tailors to make their beautiful suits, and beautiful shoes made to order. And offered them gifts that he was taken-- that he was given by Jews. He needed to bribe them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that didn't account for him to be arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Where does he have those things? They don't-- if he has got a golden cigarette case, it's not his. He should have given that to the German Reich long time before.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then--

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day he got arrested, and he got questioned. Was a story that they found some watches that were in his hiding, but it's-- the bottom line is the same. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you felt compelled to instigate the procedure for Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was only lately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was only lately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was awarded posthumously.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were three girls who worked with me, two other girls who worked with me in the cutting room. And it was my suggestion. It was equally taken up by them, except that I am in Australia, and I didn't have the survivors here, and the one in Israel has the largest amount of survivors from Bedzin. Not everybody was willing to give a testimony in his favor. Because they said he took bribes. Of course he took bribes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you all survived because of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- he needed the bribes. He supported the Jewish Resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened-- with the closure of the ghetto, what happened to your life, Kitia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the closure in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the closure of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 1st of August, 1943. It wasn't a closure. It was a total extermination of the ghetto. A total deportation. And it happened from-- in the night of between Saturday and Sunday, at the time when our watchfulness was relaxed. In our naivete, we thought, why should they disturb their own Sunday in order to surround us, and to conduct a action of extermination? They have us all the time. And only quite recently, it dawned on me, that they had us in ghetto, but they didn't have us concentrated in our place of work. They were the masses. They were the numbers. And from there, they could expect a resistance. 2,000 men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: But on Saturday, from Saturday to Sunday, everybody went to the families. One lived here, one lived there, or one lived somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the need of normalization in people living in dangerous situation is enormous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people had--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Rossner must have had an inkling, is-- something is happening. I remember when he told me-- must have been winter, '42. Now I know it was winter '42, that the Germans finally reached the Final Solution, Endlosung. Final Solution in German is Endlosung.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, terrific. I said to him, what are they going to do with us? They got, finally, a solution to our problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't understand that Final Solution meant extermination.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked at me, and he shooked his head. Didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't explain further. So anyhow, with the extermination of the ghetto, how many people were there, and what did that mean to you all?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, let me tell you that he must have had an inkling that something is brewing. Because about six weeks before the actual judenrein action, he told people working for him-- at least, he told people who worked in the building where I worked, and he lived on the second floor-- that from now on, the gates of the shop-- because those factories were called 'shop'-- will be open 24 hours a day. I mean, there's-- it's only a saying, because till 6:00, we were allowed to work. And whoever wants to bring his or her family to the shop, and stay overnight there, is welcome. To us, the closer group who were closer with him, because we saw him more often in connection with our work, he said, do not sleep in the ghetto. Quite openly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, warned you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite openly. Bring your families, and don't sleep in the ghetto. And my parents, for six weeks, did not sleep in the ghetto. At day, daybreak, they went back to their room. They washed, they cooked. They did all these things. But during the night, the ghetto was crawling with hundreds of people, and we went through a sort of a drill, which was more of a farce than anything serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What will happen in an emergency? Emergency was a distant concept. Was this an emergency? And there was a false wall built. We, in the-- A false, as in-- within the self, or a true false wall? A false wall of security, or a real wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. A false wall made from bolts and bolts of military clothes. The bolts had a circumference twice as big as this armchair. And they were stacked up in such a way that they formed a wall-- a wall. And that wall was built in such a way, then, if the alarm comes off, if there is an emergency, the people who really don't belong to the shop-- which means our parents, or the children-- go behind that wall. What will happen after, nobody even thought about it. You could not continue thinking right to the end, because ultimately, at the end was the improbable. That we will be killed. But that was unacceptable to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't-- you must've-- you didn't. How didn't you hear from other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we were totally isolated.

speaker: interviewer
text: No radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. No, no radio, no newspapers. [DRINKS] Only rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: And rumors. Rumors. Somebody said. Somebody heard. And rumors have got one common quality. If you like, you don't believe them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, just think of a person who is terminally ill. Does this person accept the ultimate verdict that his or her cancer isn't curable? Very few courageous people do. They go through extreme pain, and trauma, and stress, to be cured.

speaker: interviewer
text: In order to live. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they know. That-- they know. If they know that the ultimate end is death, why to put yourself through the mill of sufferings?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because life is the ultimate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. And this-- there was no difference. And now, imagine that you are condemned to death for no reason that you know of, except that you are a Jew. And you are not an individual. You are part of a group. And yet, you have to die. Not as an individual. And it's very difficult to look the truth in the eye, just the same as you can't look, look up to the sun, or to a bright light. You cannot. And the part of the survival kit was that this belief. Because if you would believe in the ultimate, you would give up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Kitia, the action occurred August 1, 1943. What happened, and where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night. About 12:00, 1:00, 2:00. Maybe it was 4:00. We heard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like rolling of a heavy machinery. We heard shooting. We looked out through the windows, and we saw the ghetto was as bright as a daylight. And we heard shouting. And we were stunned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rossner came running down in his pajamas, with a jacket, with tears streaming down his face, shouting, [SPEAKING GERMAN] they deceived me. They betrayed me. And sudden, he turned around, he said, what are you doing? Start doing what you were learning to do. Everybody at its post.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the one-- the people who did work in the ghetto, like me, and a few others, immediately positioned ourselves at our usual task, and the rest went behind the things. In the drill, I was allotted to be in, in a room that all the accessories were stored. All the metal accessories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Buttons, zips, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Buttons, pins, press studs, buckles, whatever. And that was a very narrow and small room. Was one window. And the shelves were not very sturdy, probably. Went from the floor to this thing. And they pushed me in that room. Of course, there was-- you couldn't put the light on, first of all. You were forbidden during the war. And secondly, you couldn't put the light on. And I was shaking as a leaf. And probably I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] touched up on the structure of shelves, stumbled, and the whole thing collapsed on me. And I was buried under metal. Literally buried under metal. And I don't think I ever wanted to wake up from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrified.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't want to wake up from a-- from it, because that was my defense. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was my defense to sleep it off, whenever I had to hide. When-- my mother told me, after the war, that during the war, every day that I woke up in that one spotlessly clean room that we had, I said, Mama, is the war over already. And she said, not yet, my child. Not yet, my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then it must have been dawn, and I extricated myself from that tomb of metal, which must have had an enormous rustle, and I couldn't find a foothold. It was so deep that I couldn't find the floor beneath it. And I was making my way towards the window. It was deathly quiet. Not a sound. Eventually I made my way through the window, and the window was covered with some sort of opaque paint, and I scratched something, and the place where we were-- by that time, we were only 50 Jews left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: The others were deported, deported, deported. Straight after judenrein-- no, by that time, no, sorry. That was in '43. So there was the whole ghetto still. And I-- we-- this department where I work was positioned in a department in a block of apartments, which was called in Poland blocks. And civilians lived there. And our department occupied-- the cutting room occupied, the basement, and offices, the ground floor. And Rossner had his flat either on the first or on the second floor. I don't know. And we were just opposite the station. And I glued my eye to this. And I saw a sea of human faces. Not a sound coming there. The distance was less that from one side of street and here. Not a sound. And I didn't know whether it's real or not. And gradually, I recognized some faces. And they were immobile. And as I had a very narrow field of vision, I had the impression that they are only faces, because I didn't see the bodies. And the faces were immobile. The heads didn't move. It was an eerie feeling. And then, obviously, I have fainted again. And the next thing that woke me out-- up was, I fainted, and I think I induced the fainting on me. And the next thing that woke me up was banging and shouting, and banging at the floor-- at the door of that room, and trying to prise them open. But, of course, they couldn't, because it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --blocked with mountain of metal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was given up. And what actually woke me up were voices. I don't remember whose voices, but I knew them. In German, she must be there. She is there. And they pushed the door, and they fished me out from there. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the-- a very telling situation, and particularly telling, as far as human behavior is concerned, that by that time, August '43, politically, Italy was defeated. Germans did not reenter Italy yet. Mussolini was hanged already. And here was talk that Rossner intends to open another shop where only hundred families will go there. He already knew that he can't save the whole ghetto. And we were among the families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father said, Italy's defeated, the war is towards the end, and I have to go willingly into a labor camp? Because that was supposed to send him. And, of course, mother and myself said, we are going. We, all of us, were-- felt so protected by Rossner, and had such a unlimited and unqualified faith in his goodwill, that if that was his suggestion, I didn't see it, at that time yet, as a part of a plan to save Jews. And I wasn't interested why. I was quite happy with the fact that we are included in that plan. But your instinct must have given you some direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instinct are only serving you the way you wanted them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it comes to saving your life, your instinct tells you to run left, and not to right. But when it comes to life itself, you don't obey your instinct. You trust-- in my case, I trusted my instinct into somebody's else hands. The religious people trust in God. A lot of us trusted that Rossner's intentions are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Honorable.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, we had to be packed, take as much as you can, you know, of course. And on Sunday morning, a platform was supposed to come. A truck to take our luggage. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- I and many others had distinct orders from Rossner not to sleep in ghetto. I couldn't tell that to my parents. I said, we shouldn't sleep in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said, you said that they were sleeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. But that was Saturday, Sunday. You see? And a lot of people on Saturday felt protected, because next day is Sunday. Nothing will happen on Sunday. Why should the German do something on Sunday and disturb their day of rest? So what happened is, that while we were sleeping in the ghetto, the underground moved into our rooms, and I-- I had friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish underground. It was a small group of people, of my friends. So they hid in the cellars, and by 6:00, when we were marched off to our place of work, to the factory, which was just outside the ghetto, they moved into our room. It didn't arise any suspicions, because they were people my age, and very often, you know, people stay overnight. And they used our room for forging papers, preparing people who were ready to go on the Aryan side, telling them what place in the pictures, telling them about facts of life-- how to walk, how to look, what not to do. Don't turn around. Don't turn about your shoulders. Don't run. How to dress, how to say prayers, what are the latest Polish catchwords, and so on, and so on, and so on. Apart of having a very primitive machine, where they duplicated, they forged papers, and so on. And the time was quarter to six, and my father said, I am not going. The last night, I want to spend on my bed. I was desperate. More than anything else, I feared my father's ire. And I said, I am going. And my mother said, if she's going, I'm going. And my father said, you are blackmailing me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you were.
